home.subtitle: An authentic experience aboard vintage carriages. Travel through National Parks and the most beautiful villages of the Central Apennines.
home.sponsorsAria: Partners and sponsors of Transiberiana d'Abruzzo
home.heroImageAlt: Historic train crossing a viaduct among the green mountains of Abruzzo

hero.subtitle: Born as a major mountain railway challenge, the Sulmona-Isernia line was inaugurated on September 18, 1897 and returned to life on May 17, 2014 as one of Italy's most iconic tourist railways.

timeline.title: Construction begins
timeline.desc: In 1888 work started on the Sulmona-Isernia line, created to connect the inland Apennines of Abruzzo and Molise with a new east-west railway route.

popup.title: Building through mountains and villages
popup.intro: At the end of the nineteenth century many towns in the central Apennines were difficult to reach, and moving people or goods meant slow journeys on harsh roads.
paragraphs: The Italian state therefore chose to build an inland railway between Sulmona and Isernia, independent from the coastal lines and able to connect towns such as Palena, Rivisondoli and Pescocostanzo.

The project was extremely complex: gradients had to remain manageable, curves could not be too tight and the route still needed to stay close to settlements, requiring tunnels, masonry works, bridges and viaducts.
Workers faced extreme conditions, with deep winter snow and exhausting summer heat, turning the line into one of the clearest examples of Apennine railway engineering.
highlights: 128 km original route
59 tunnels
bridges and viaducts along the full line

timeline.imageAlt: Historic locomotive at a station, evoking the first years of the Sulmona-Isernia line

timeline.title: The line is inaugurated
timeline.desc: On September 18, 1897 the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo was inaugurated with a celebratory train, immediately becoming a crucial link for people, goods and mountain communities.

popup.title: The day the railway changed the mountains
popup.intro: The official inauguration took place on September 18, 1897 with a celebratory train travelling from Castellammare Adriatico to Naples across the whole mountain section.
paragraphs: Authorities, engineers, workers and local communities all took part, and the railway was immediately seen as a symbol of progress and modernity for isolated mountain areas.
The Rivisondoli-Pescocostanzo pass reached 1,268.82 metres above sea level, a record for Italy's standard-gauge railway network for many years.

In the early years the full Sulmona-Isernia journey took about 5 hours and 55 minutes; by 1938 the same route could be covered in 3 hours and 33 minutes thanks to better rolling stock and infrastructure.
highlights: September 18, 1897
1,268.82 m at the Rivisondoli-Pescocostanzo pass
5h 55m in the first years of service

timeline.imageAlt: Historic postcard of Palena station around the opening years of the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo

timeline.title: War and destruction
timeline.desc: During the Second World War the line was heavily damaged along the Gustav Line, with bridges, viaducts and tracks destroyed during the German retreat.

popup.title: The railway on the Gustav Line
popup.intro: During the war the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo had strong strategic value because it crossed the Apennines far from the main coastal routes.
paragraphs: In November 1943, during the German retreat, bridges and viaducts were destroyed, parts of the line were mined and many tracks were damaged to block Allied movements.
Places such as Palena, Rivisondoli and Castel di Sangro were left isolated while the surrounding territory also suffered bombings and severe logistical disruption.
Recovery was slow: the Sulmona-Castel di Sangro section reopened in 1955, while the full line to Isernia returned only on November 9, 1960.
highlights: November 1943: wartime destruction
1955: Sulmona-Castel di Sangro reopens
November 9, 1960: full reactivation

timeline.imageAlt: Locomotive among destroyed railway structures during the Second World War

timeline.title: Transformation and decline
timeline.desc: After the war the line still served passengers and freight, but cars, buses and the depopulation of mountain villages progressively made it less central.

popup.title: Modernisation followed by decline
popup.intro: After reconstruction the railway still experienced an active phase, with passenger services, freight trains and an important role in the mountain economy.
paragraphs: Freight trains continued to carry livestock and sheep, partly replacing traditional transhumance and keeping direct links with the Tavoliere delle Puglie.
At the same time ALn 668 diesel railcars cut travel times sharply and Pescara-Napoli services could reach about 2 hours and 47 minutes.
But the spread of private cars, improved roads and the depopulation of mountain villages steadily reduced railway traffic, paving the way for the decline that became clear by the 1990s.
highlights: freight trains for sheep and livestock
ALn 668 railcars in service
Pescara-Napoli in about 2h 47m

timeline.imageAlt: ALn railcar stopped at Castel di Sangro in 1976, symbol of the line's transition years

timeline.title: Service is suspended
timeline.desc: Between October 2010 and December 2011, first the Castel di Sangro-Carpinone section and then the Sulmona-Castel di Sangro section were suspended, stopping regular service.

popup.title: The end of regular service
popup.intro: The suspension arrived after a long weakening of the service, caused by low passenger numbers, high costs and shrinking investment in local rail transport.
paragraphs: In October 2010 traffic stopped between Castel di Sangro and Carpinone; in December 2011 the last active section between Sulmona and Castel di Sangro was also closed.
Up to that point the line was still used by local and interregional trains, in some cases classified as the Pescara-Napoli Espresso, with journeys of about five hours.
The closure caused strong disappointment among residents and railway enthusiasts, but it also sparked the mobilisation that later led to the tourist rebirth of the route.
highlights: October 2010: stop on Castel di Sangro-Carpinone
December 2011: stop on Sulmona-Castel di Sangro
grassroots mobilisation supported by Le Rotaie

timeline.imageAlt: Tracks of the historic line disappearing into the valley, recalling the years of service suspension

timeline.title: Tourist rebirth
timeline.desc: After the success of the first historic trains promoted by Le Rotaie, on May 17, 2014 the line was reborn as a tourist railway with Fondazione FS's 'Binari Senza Tempo' project.

popup.title: From grassroots rescue to the Railway of the Parks
popup.intro: Immediately after the suspension, associations, enthusiasts and local communities recognised the extraordinary tourist potential of the line.
paragraphs: The first experimental historic train ran on March 4, 2012 thanks to the Le Rotaie association and drew a large public response, helped by the snowy mountain scenery.
The real turning point came on May 17, 2014 with Fondazione FS Italiane's Binari Senza Tempo project, which formally dedicated the Sulmona-Isernia line to tourist and cultural use.

In 2017 law 128/2017 officially recognised it as an Italian tourist railway; more recently works supported also by PNRR funds improved tracks, bridges and stations while preserving the line's historic character.
highlights: March 4, 2012: first historic train
May 17, 2014: Binari Senza Tempo
2017: official tourist railway recognition

timeline.imageAlt: Tourist train of the Ferrovia dei Parchi crossing woods and mountain scenery

stations.name: Sulmona
stations.desc: The city of sugared almonds and Ovid. Starting point of the historic itinerary.

stations.name: Campo di Giove
stations.desc: At the foot of the Majella, framed by dense woods and breathtaking landscapes.

stations.name: Palena
stations.desc: An isolated station in Quarto Santa Chiara, a realm of untamed nature.

stations.name: Roccaraso
stations.desc: The highest station on the line, famous for mountain tourism in both winter and summer.

stations.name: Castel di Sangro
stations.desc: Town of water and fly fishing, a key hub of the upper Sangro Valley.

stops.title: Stations along the journey
stops.subtitle: After the history, the route continues through villages and plateaus: every stop is a moment to enjoy slowly.

cta.title: Buy your ticket
cta.body: Leave your email and we will let you know as soon as bookings open for the next departures.

form.label: Email
form.success: Thanks! We will let you know as soon as bookings open.

storia.subtitle: Each stop opens as a visual, narrative and immersive chapter of the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo. From Sulmona to Isernia, the route becomes a sequence of towns, landscapes, traditions and local flavors.
storia.panelBody: The redesign replaces the classic descriptive window with a full experience: editorial hero, chapters, visual cues and a narrative rhythm across the towns of the route.

cards.label: Chapter 01
cards.title: Sulmona
cards.summary: A medieval town in the Peligna Valley, famous for its artisanal sugared almonds and for the medieval aqueduct with 21 arches crossing the historic center.
cards.story: Sulmona has very ancient origins. In pre-Roman times the area was inhabited by the Italic people of the Peligni and with the Roman conquest it became a municipium. Today it preserves a historic center rich in monuments, popular traditions, gastronomy and artistic heritage.

experience.intro: A town in the Peligna Valley with pre-Roman and Roman origins, famous for artisanal sugared almonds and for the medieval aqueduct with 21 arches.
experience.body: The town preserves historic churches and palaces and each year hosts cultural and traditional events celebrating its history and local craftsmanship.
experience.quote: Sulmona brings together ancient history, popular traditions, gastronomy and artistic heritage.

stats.label: Traditions
stats.value: Giostra Cavalleresca and Madonna che Scappa in Piazza

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: In pre-Roman times the area was inhabited by the Italic people of the Peligni. With the Roman conquest, the city became a municipium and developed thanks to its strategic position between the Adriatic and the inner Apennines.
chapters.pullout: Publius Ovidius Naso, one of the most important authors of Roman literature, was born here.

chapters.title: Patron saint and monuments
chapters.body: The symbol of the city is Sulmona's medieval aqueduct, built in the thirteenth century and formed by a long series of arches crossing Piazza Garibaldi, the main square. Among the most important religious buildings is San Panfilo Cathedral, dedicated to the patron saint of the city, together with numerous historic churches and old palaces.
chapters.pullout: The historic center preserves monuments and artistic evidence among the most representative of the city.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Among the most important traditions is the Giostra Cavalleresca of Sulmona, a historical reenactment held every summer in which the riders of the city's districts compete in a tournament inspired by the Middle Ages. Another very famous celebration is the Madonna che Scappa in Piazza, held on Easter morning.
chapters.pullout: Each year the city hosts cultural and traditional events celebrating its history and local craftsmanship.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Sulmona is famous throughout Italy for the production of sugared almonds, sweets made of almonds covered in sugar, produced by hand and often arranged in decorative flower compositions. Local cuisine also includes dishes based on legumes such as lentils and chickpeas, maccheroni alla chitarra, lamb and sheep meat, and traditional desserts such as torcinelli and calcionetti abruzzesi.
chapters.pullout: These flavors reflect a rural tradition that combines simplicity, local ingredients and ancient preparation techniques.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Thanks to its position in the Peligna Valley and its proximity to the mountains and natural parks of the Apennines, Sulmona is today also an important starting point for excursions and nature tourism.
chapters.pullout: Its location between the Peligna Valley and the Apennines makes Sulmona a natural stop between the city, the mountains and routes through the landscape.

gallery.alt: View of Sulmona with the medieval aqueduct and square
gallery.caption: Medieval aqueduct and Piazza Garibaldi.
Artisanal confetti and floral decorative compositions.

gallery.alt: Madonna che Scappa in Piazza during the Easter celebration in Sulmona
gallery.caption: Madonna che Scappa in Piazza and civic celebrations.

rituals.title: Giostra Cavalleresca
rituals.desc: Summer historical reenactment in which the riders of the city's districts compete in a tournament inspired by the Middle Ages.

rituals.title: Madonna che Scappa in Piazza
rituals.desc: Easter celebration in which the statue of the Madonna runs toward the statue of the risen Christ among the crowd gathered in the square.

rituals.title: Artisanal sugared almonds
rituals.desc: Ancient artisanal tradition linked to sugar-coated almonds and decorative compositions shaped like flowers.

closing.title: History, traditions, gastronomy and landscape
closing.body: Sulmona brings together ancient history, popular traditions, gastronomy and artistic heritage, making it one of the most interesting cultural centers in Abruzzo.

detailGallery.alt: Detail of the arches of Sulmona's medieval aqueduct
detailGallery.caption: The sequence of 21 arches marks the walk along the edge of the square.

detailGallery.alt: Piazza Garibaldi in Sulmona opening toward the mountains
detailGallery.caption: Piazza Garibaldi opens as a broad civic space between houses, fountain and mountain backdrop.

detailGallery.alt: Statue of Ovid in the center of Sulmona
detailGallery.caption: The memory of Ovid reappears among public buildings and civic architecture.

cards.label: Chapter 02
cards.title: Campo di Giove
cards.summary: Mountain village in the Majella Park, around 1100 meters above sea level, among beech woods, trails and wide-open Apennine views.
cards.story: Campo di Giove has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to transhumance and commercial routes. Today it preserves stone architecture, local celebrations and a strong bond with the landscape of the Majella.

experience.intro: A mountain village in the Majella Park, around 1100 meters above sea level, among beech woods, trails and Apennine landscapes.
experience.body: Campo di Giove has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center tied to transhumance. Today it preserves paved streets, stone houses, small squares and a strong natural vocation.
experience.quote: Campo di Giove brings together mountain memory, local traditions and the landscapes of the Majella.

stats.label: Monuments
stats.value: San Vincenzo church and local stone

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Campo di Giove has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center along routes linked to trade and transhumance. Its history is closely tied to pastoral life and to the landscape of the Majella.
chapters.pullout: The village grew along routes of exchange and pastoral movement between mountain slopes and valleys.

chapters.title: Historic center and monuments
chapters.body: The village preserves paved streets, stone houses and small squares that retain the character of a mountain settlement. Among the most important religious buildings is San Vincenzo church, alongside smaller chapels scattered through the historic fabric.
chapters.pullout: The urban fabric preserves the compact scale of the mountain centers of the Majella.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Village life is marked by patron feasts, processions and local fairs that keep community gatherings alive. These celebrations accompany the civil and religious calendar of the village.
chapters.pullout: The local calendar brings together devotion, public gathering and the memory of the village.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes sheep and goat cheeses, cured meats, honey and mountain sweets. Among the best-known dishes are homemade maccheroni, gnocchi, legume soups, roasted lamb and goat and bread baked in a wood-fired oven.
chapters.pullout: Mountain cooking comes from livestock, simple products and domestic preparation.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Thanks to its position in the Majella Park, Campo di Giove is today a point of reference for trekking, walks and nature itineraries through woods and mountains.
chapters.pullout: The Majella, the beech woods and the trails make the village a natural gateway to hiking.

gallery.alt: View of Campo di Giove at the foot of the Majella
gallery.caption: Mountain landscape and village profile in the Majella Park.

gallery.alt: Church of San Vincenzo in Campo di Giove
gallery.caption: Church of San Vincenzo and the Majella landscape.

gallery.alt: Stone stairs and rooftops in the historic village of Campo di Giove
gallery.caption: Stone lanes, masonry houses and village views.

rituals.title: Patron feasts
rituals.desc: The religious celebrations gather the community at the main moments of the year.

rituals.title: Processions
rituals.desc: Processions accompany celebrations and keep the religious dimension of the village alive.

rituals.title: Local fairs
rituals.desc: Public gatherings connect tradition, encounter and mountain village life.

closing.title: Majella, stone and mountain life
closing.body: Campo di Giove preserves the memory of transhumance, stone architecture, local traditions and a strong vocation for the landscape of the Majella.

detailGallery.alt: Campo di Giove in autumn among slopes and village houses
detailGallery.caption: The village lies at the foot of the Majella among autumn colors and upland fields.

detailGallery.alt: Stone stairway in the historic center of Campo di Giove
detailGallery.caption: Stairs, arches and masonry walls preserve the compact rhythm of the mountain village.

detailGallery.alt: Sundial on a stone house in Campo di Giove
detailGallery.caption: Stone details and sundials recall the slower pace of mountain life.

cards.label: Chapter 03
cards.title: Palena
cards.summary: Mountain village near the Forchetta pass, between the Majella, the high plateaus and one of the route's most panoramic railway points.
cards.story: Palena has ancient and medieval origins and developed as a pastoral and agricultural center. Today it maintains a strong bond with San Falco, with its railway station and with the upland landscape around the village.

experience.intro: A mountain village tied to the Majella and the high plateaus, near the Forchetta pass and one of the most panoramic railway points on the route.
experience.body: Palena has ancient and medieval origins and developed as a pastoral and agricultural center. Today it maintains a close link with San Falco, with the railway station and with the high-altitude landscape.
experience.quote: Palena brings together devotion, mountain landscape and railway memory.

stats.label: Monuments
stats.value: San Falco church and the station

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Palena has ancient and medieval origins and developed as a mountain center tied to pastoral life and agriculture. Its history is linked to pastures, trails and upland rural activity.
chapters.pullout: The village grew within a mountain economy shaped by livestock and agricultural work.

chapters.title: Patron saint and monuments
chapters.body: Among the most important places is San Falco church, linked to the patron saint of the town. Another identity landmark is the railway station on the high plateaus, connected to one of the most panoramic stretches of the journey.
chapters.pullout: San Falco and the station tell the religious and railway identity of Palena.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Village life is still marked by patron feasts, processions and historical reenactments that keep alive the relationship between community, religious memory and the story of the town.
chapters.pullout: Public celebrations renew the link between the community and its history.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local flavors include homemade maccheroni, legume soups, roasts, cheeses, honey and chestnuts. Traditional sweets include mountain zeppole, almond and walnut sweets and bread baked in a wood-fired oven.
chapters.pullout: Palena's table reflects the mountain landscape and its simplest and most enduring products.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Its proximity to the Majella, to the plateaus and to mountain trails makes Palena a starting point for excursions, walks and upland nature routes.
chapters.pullout: Majella, plateaus and trails make Palena a stop of landscape and mountain breathing space.

gallery.alt: View of Palena among woods and the ridges of the Majella
gallery.caption: Palena between the Majella, uplands and the village landscape.

gallery.alt: Church of San Falco in Palena
gallery.caption: Church of San Falco and places of local devotion.

gallery.alt: Historic stone fountain in the center of Palena
gallery.caption: Stone fountains and views of the historic center.

rituals.title: Patron feasts
rituals.desc: Patron celebrations mark the most felt moments of village life.

rituals.title: Processions
rituals.desc: Processions keep alive the religious and public dimension of the community.

rituals.title: Historical reenactments
rituals.desc: Historical events bring episodes and symbols of local history back into the present.

closing.title: Palena between devotion and landscape
closing.body: Palena brings together mountain roots, local faith, railway memory and a cuisine tied to upland life.

detailGallery.alt: Winter view of Palena among valley and mountains
detailGallery.caption: The village appears gathered in the valley, surrounded by ridges and upland scenery.

detailGallery.alt: Archway and houses in the center of Palena
detailGallery.caption: Arches, climbs and attached houses preserve the old rhythm of the historic center.
Water and vegetation accompany the edges of the village toward the mountain landscape.

cards.label: Chapter 04
cards.title: Roccaraso
cards.summary: A mountain town in Alto Sangro, between winter sports, summer trails and high-altitude open landscapes.
cards.story: Roccaraso has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to commerce and local activity. Today it is one of the main tourist destinations of the Apennines, visited in winter and in summer.

experience.intro: A mountain town in Alto Sangro, known for winter tourism and for outdoor activities that animate the town in summer too.
experience.body: Roccaraso has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to commerce. Today it combines religious and civil architecture, village traditions and a strong tourist vocation.
experience.quote: Roccaraso brings together mountain architecture, village traditions and upland tourism.

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Roccaraso has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to local trade and the activities of the territory. Its growth is connected to upland life and to the routes of Alto Sangro.
chapters.pullout: The history of the town begins from the encounter between mountain settlement, exchange and high-altitude life.

chapters.title: Monuments
chapters.body: Among the most important buildings is Santa Maria Assunta church, together with noble palaces that testify to the urban history of the town. These places define the historic face of Roccaraso.
chapters.pullout: Church and palaces tell the passage between religious, civil and architectural memory.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Religious feasts and village festivals keep Roccaraso's community calendar alive. In these moments, typical local products also take center stage, linked to local culture and conviviality.
chapters.pullout: The town's celebrations bring together the religious dimension, public gathering and products of the territory.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes homemade pasta, soups, roasts and stews, cheeses and honey. Among the sweets are zeppole, dried-fruit tarts and bread baked in a wood-fired oven, in a table shaped by the mountains and by seasonality.
chapters.pullout: Roccaraso's cuisine comes from simple ingredients, slow time and mountain flavors.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Roccaraso occupies a central position in the landscapes of Alto Sangro and today is one of the main bases for winter sports, summer trekking and mountain-bike routes.
chapters.pullout: The geography of Alto Sangro makes Roccaraso a natural stop for the mountains in every season.

gallery.alt: Snowy Roccaraso among woods and mountains
gallery.caption: Roccaraso between the mountain town and the landscapes of Alto Sangro.

gallery.alt: View of Roccaraso with viaduct and church
gallery.caption: Village architecture and high-altitude railway scenery.

gallery.alt: Center of Roccaraso with square, flowers and daily life
gallery.caption: Mountain tourism and town-center life between winter and summer.

rituals.title: Religious feasts
rituals.desc: Religious celebrations keep the collective dimension of the town alive.

rituals.title: Village festivals
rituals.desc: Festivals bring together public gathering, local cuisine and mountain identity.

rituals.title: Typical products
rituals.desc: Products of the territory accompany festivities and moments of local conviviality.

closing.title: Roccaraso between architecture and tourism
closing.body: Roccaraso weaves together mountain history, local architecture, village traditions and activities tied to the seasons of Alto Sangro.

detailGallery.alt: Snowy view of Roccaraso among woods and hotels
detailGallery.caption: Snow, woods and slopes immediately reveal the town's winter vocation.
Even minor religious buildings contribute to the architectural face of the village.

detailGallery.alt: Ski slope and chairlift in Roccaraso
detailGallery.caption: The slopes express Roccaraso's direct link with snow tourism.

cards.label: Chapter 05
cards.title: Castel di Sangro
cards.summary: A center of Alto Sangro between fortified origins, stone streets, places of art and a strong cultural vocation.
cards.story: Castel di Sangro was born as a fortified medieval settlement around the castle and developed as the center of the valley. Today it preserves lanes, squares, religious monuments and a strong cultural life.

experience.intro: A center of Alto Sangro with fortified origins, stone streets, religious monuments and one of the main cultural poles of the valley.
experience.body: Castel di Sangro was born as a medieval nucleus around the castle and developed as the center of the valley. Today it combines lanes, squares, sacred art, a museum and cultural activity.
experience.quote: Castel di Sangro interweaves medieval memory, cultural life and the cuisine of Alto Sangro.

stats.label: Monuments
stats.value: Santa Maria Assunta and the gallery

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Castel di Sangro was born as a fortified medieval settlement developed around the castle. From this nucleus the center of the valley took shape, becoming a place of passage and urban life in Alto Sangro.
chapters.pullout: The first layout of the town is linked to defensive functions and control of the territory.

chapters.title: Monuments and historic center
chapters.body: The historic center preserves lanes, squares and stone architecture that define the face of the town. Among the most important places are Santa Maria Assunta church and the Patiniana Gallery, a cultural reference for the city.
chapters.pullout: Among stone lanes and places of art, the center tells the urban role of Castel di Sangro.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: The life of the town is accompanied by patron feasts, historical reenactments and local fairs that renew collective memory and the link with the territory.
chapters.pullout: Public celebrations keep civic identity, local history and community participation together.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes maccheroni alla chitarra with lamb or wild boar sauce, tacconelli, pecorini, guanciale and other cured meats. Among the sweets are ricotta and chestnut zeppole, together with local bread and honey.
chapters.pullout: The table of Castel di Sangro combines traditional pasta, meats, cheeses and sweets of the valley.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Its position in the upper Sangro Valley and its proximity to the mountains make Castel di Sangro a reference center for tourism and for the cultural life of the valley.
chapters.pullout: Between valley, mountain and town, Castel di Sangro keeps a hinge role in the territory.

gallery.alt: Panorama of Castel di Sangro seen from above
gallery.caption: Castel di Sangro between the historic center and the Alto Sangro landscape.

gallery.alt: Facade of Santa Maria Assunta in Castel di Sangro
gallery.caption: Santa Maria Assunta and the city's religious heritage.

gallery.alt: Square in Castel di Sangro with fountain and festive flags
gallery.caption: Squares, fountains and public moments in the town center.

rituals.title: Patron feasts
rituals.desc: Patron celebrations continue to mark the public rhythm of the town.

rituals.title: Historical reenactments
rituals.desc: Reenactment events bring episodes and atmospheres of local history back to the center.

rituals.title: Local fairs
rituals.desc: Fairs keep alive the relationship between town, territory and local production.

closing.title: Castel di Sangro between culture and valley
closing.body: Castel di Sangro preserves medieval origins, places of art, urban traditions and a cuisine that reflects the landscape of Alto Sangro.

detailGallery.alt: View of the Alto Sangro valley near Castel di Sangro
detailGallery.caption: The city sits within a broad system of valley, fields and Apennine ridges.

detailGallery.alt: Urban view of Castel di Sangro seen from below
detailGallery.caption: From the valley floor emerge the urban profile and churches that mark the historic center.

detailGallery.alt: Castel di Sangro illuminated at night
detailGallery.caption: At night the village highlights facades, climbs and monumental focal points.

cards.label: Chapter 06
cards.title: Isernia
cards.summary: An ancient city of Molise, between the Paleolithic site of Isernia La Pineta, historic monuments and a strong urban identity.
cards.story: Isernia sinks its roots into the Paleolithic and crosses the Samnite, Roman and medieval eras. Today it is a cultural and administrative center that brings together monuments, traditions, craftsmanship and gastronomy.

experience.intro: An ancient city of Molise, known for the Paleolithic site of Isernia La Pineta and for its historic center with churches, palaces, museums and monumental fountains.
experience.body: Isernia crosses the Samnite, Roman and medieval eras and today keeps a strategic role from a cultural and administrative point of view, together with a strong artisanal and gastronomic identity.
experience.quote: Isernia brings together millenary roots, monuments, traditions and the flavors of inland Molise.

stats.label: Traditions
stats.value: Holy Week and the feast of Saint Peter

stats.label: Flavors
stats.value: Cavatelli, cured meats and honey sweets

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Isernia sinks its roots into the Paleolithic, as shown by the site of Isernia La Pineta. This earliest phase is followed by the Samnite and Roman eras, while in the Middle Ages the city developed as a religious and commercial center.
chapters.pullout: From prehistory to the Middle Ages, Isernia preserves one of the broadest historical stratifications of the route.

chapters.title: Monuments and historic center
chapters.body: Among the most important places are San Pietro Apostolo Cathedral, San Francesco church, Palazzo San Francesco, Fontana Fraterna and Piazza Andrea d'Isernia. These spaces define the urban identity of the city.
chapters.pullout: Cathedral, fountain, palaces and squares compose the historic profile of the city center.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Among the most deeply felt traditions are the Holy Week processions, the feast of Saint Peter and summer food fairs. Alongside them are iron craftsmanship, textiles and markets that keep the urban character of the city alive.
chapters.pullout: The traditions of Isernia connect devotion, markets and artisanal knowledge.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes cavatelli with turnip greens or sausage sauce, potato-and-ricotta gnocchi, tagliatelle with lamb or goat ragout, Molise pecorino, soppressata and capocollo. Among the sweets are ricotta fritters, zeppole, almond-and-honey biscuits, homemade bread and mountain honey.
chapters.pullout: The table of Isernia brings together traditional pasta, cured meats, dairy products and sweets of inland Molise.

chapters.title: Current role
chapters.body: Today Isernia is a strategic city from the cultural and administrative point of view. Its contemporary identity brings together millenary history, medieval and baroque architecture, living traditions and gastronomy.
chapters.pullout: In the present, Isernia is recognized as a city of history, urban functions and widespread memory.

gallery.alt: Panorama of Isernia in the landscape of inland Molise
gallery.caption: Isernia between the historic center and the landscape of inland Molise.
Historic-center views among palaces, alleys and towers.

gallery.alt: Piazza Celestino V in central Isernia
gallery.caption: Piazza Celestino V and spaces of the historic city.

rituals.title: Holy Week
rituals.desc: The Holy Week processions are among the most deeply felt moments of the city's calendar.

rituals.title: Feast of Saint Peter
rituals.desc: The feast dedicated to Saint Peter accompanies one of the most recognizable religious events of the city.

rituals.title: Markets and craftsmanship
rituals.desc: Ironwork, textiles and markets keep the artisanal and commercial tradition of Isernia alive.

closing.title: Isernia between prehistory and historic city
closing.body: Isernia brings together the Paleolithic site, monuments of the center, city traditions and a cuisine that tells the story of inland Molise.

detailGallery.alt: Historic lane in Isernia with tower in the background
detailGallery.caption: The lanes of the center guide the eye toward towers, arches and passages of the old city.

detailGallery.alt: Another historic-center view in Isernia among balconies and bell tower
detailGallery.caption: Balconies, lamps and bell towers define the fine-grained texture of the old center.

detailGallery.alt: View of the San Felice quarter in Isernia
detailGallery.caption: Small squares and domestic fronts reveal the everyday dimension of the city.

overlay.ritualsTitle: What the city puts back on stage
overlay.finalNote: The first chapter is a threshold: from here history gains rhythm, material and landscape.
overlay.placeholderNote: For now this city opens in a compact format, with a narrative summary designed to accompany the journey.

stops.title: Stops
stops.subtitle: Explore the historic stations along Italy's highest and most scenic railway, a ribbon of steel stitching together national parks and nature reserves.

stations.name: Sulmona
stations.desc: The city of sugared almonds and Ovid. Starting point of the historic itinerary.

stations.name: Campo di Giove
stations.desc: At the foot of the Majella, framed by dense woods and breathtaking landscapes.

stations.name: Palena
stations.desc: An isolated station in Quarto Santa Chiara, a realm of untamed nature.

stations.name: Roccaraso
stations.desc: The highest station on the line, famous for mountain tourism in both winter and summer.

stations.name: Castel di Sangro
stations.desc: Town of water and fly fishing, a key hub of the upper Sangro Valley.

activities.title: What to do during the stops
activities.subtitle: Choose a stop, switch season and explore an itinerary that fits the real amount of time you actually have.
activities.note: Timings and stop lengths are indicative and may change depending on the historic service.
activities.stationSelectorHint: Pick a stop to switch content, images and itinerary suggestions.
activities.stationListAria: Select a stop along the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo route

seasonCopy.spring: Villages, meadows and longer light: the best season for stretching your walks.
seasonCopy.summer: The middle of the day is warmer, but mountain air and shade make the stops pleasant.
seasonCopy.autumn: Foliage, local produce and softer light make the trip feel more narrative and more gastronomic.
seasonCopy.winter: Snow, warm interiors and iconic views: it pays to alternate scenery and sheltered places.

stops.name: Sulmona
stops.timeHint: Best before departure or on the way back, with different routes depending on the light and time of day.
stops.desc: The city of sugared almonds and Ovid: first gateway into the journey and the natural place to close the day among squares, museums and workshops.
stops.heroAlt: Panoramic view of Sulmona with its historic center at the foot of the mountains of Abruzzo.

schedule.note: Indicative timings only: in the morning it is better to stay compact, while on the way back porticoes, museums and dinner in town work best.

seasonContext.spring: Clear light and mild weather make Sulmona ideal for moving between squares, the aqueduct and open-air aperitifs.
seasonContext.summer: Visits work best from late afternoon onwards, looking for shade between Corso Ovidio, Porta Napoli and Piazza XX Settembre.
seasonContext.autumn: Museums, workshops and signature products such as sugared almonds and red garlic reveal the city at a slower pace.
seasonContext.winter: When the train returns it is often already dark, so Corso Ovidio's porticoes, the Annunziata complex and lit shop windows become the best option.

highlights: SS. Annunziata complex with civic museums and Roman domus.
Piazza Garibaldi, the medieval aqueduct and Fontana del Vecchio.
Corso Ovidio, Porta Napoli and Piazza XX Settembre with the statue of Ovid.
Pelino Museum of Sugared Almond Art.
Cathedral of San Panfilo with its early medieval crypt.
stops.practicalNote: Sulmona is the most flexible stop of the journey: stay inside the historic center if time is tight, while Badia Morronese and the Hermitage of Sant'Onofrio only make sense if you arrive much earlier or spend the night in town.
howItWorks: Before boarding, keep to a short loop through the main squares and streets.
On the return leg, especially in winter, favor museums, porticoes and venues in the center.
Sugared almonds and artisan shops are the easiest souvenir choice without moving far from the station area.

plans.label: Short stop
plans.bestFor: Before departure or a quick return stop
plans.summary: A compact loop through the city's icons, with an easy sweet break built in.
plans.logistics: Keeping to Piazza Garibaldi, Corso Ovidio and the Annunziata complex means everything stays within walking distance.
plans.mealTip: Artisan sugared almonds, a light aperitif or gelato in the center are the easiest pauses to fit in.

spring: Piazza Garibaldi and the 1256 medieval aqueduct, with a short pass by Fontana del Vecchio.
Walk up to the Annunziata complex and enjoy an outdoor aperitif with cured meats and local pecorino.
summer: Start at Porta Napoli and follow a shaded route along Corso Ovidio.
Stop in Piazza XX Settembre beneath the statue of Ovid, then grab gelato before leaving.
autumn: Visit the Pelino Museum of Sugared Almond Art, the city's sweetest memory lane.
Walk to Santa Maria della Tomba and browse shops for Sulmona red garlic or other local produce.
winter: The Annunziata complex, civic museums and Roman domus are the best refuge in the coldest hours.
Corso Ovidio's porticoes, lit shop windows and a quick call at San Panfilo close the return stop well.

plans.label: Relaxed stop
plans.bestFor: Arriving early or returning without rushing
plans.summary: More time to combine culture, a slower walk and a seated break.
plans.logistics: You can add a museum or a major church without leaving the historic-center perimeter.
plans.mealTip: With more time, a central trattoria or a warm dinner on the way back makes sense.

spring: Take the Annunziata complex, the aqueduct and the old center at a slow pace, leaving room for photos and architectural details.
If you are already in town earlier, Badia Morronese and the Hermitage of Sant'Onofrio are the right extra cultural detour.
summer: Pair the Roman domus with an evening walk along Corso Ovidio once the sun drops.
Spend longer among squares, workshops and outdoor tables in the heart of town.
autumn: Combine the Pelino museum, Santa Maria della Tomba and gastronomic shopping in artisan stores.
Sugared almonds, Montepulciano d'Abruzzo wine and local specialties tell the season particularly well.
winter: Visit San Panfilo Cathedral, its early medieval crypt and the Annunziata museums.
Finish with dinner in an osteria serving warm dishes such as chitarra pasta with lamb ragu or a legume soup.

stops.name: Campo di Giove
stops.timeHint: The village is compact: a simple focused walk is enough to experience it properly.
stops.desc: At the foot of the Majella, Campo di Giove mixes a medieval layout, stone alleyways and the rhythm of a mountain village that still feels intact.
stops.heroAlt: Facade of the mother church of Campo di Giove, between pale stone and mountain sky.

schedule.note: Time is limited but enough for a loop through the old village, a cultural stop and a quick food break.

seasonContext.spring: The weather stays cool and pleasant: the best season for reading carved portals and architectural details.
seasonContext.summer: Altitude keeps the air light, so the shaded alleyways and flowered balconies become the natural set of the visit.
seasonContext.autumn: The fortified village plays against Majella foliage and stronger seasonal flavors.
seasonContext.winter: Snow and stone make Campo di Giove highly scenic: combine a short walk with one warm indoor stop.

highlights: Church of Sant'Eustachio with carved choir and religious works.
Rue and stone portals with sculpted masks.
Casa Quaranta, among the oldest houses in town.
Palazzo Nanni and the memory of transhumance.
Piazza Duval and the perimeter of the fortified village.
stops.practicalNote: Campo di Giove is best visited on foot: the center is compact and it is wiser not to stretch far beyond the old village if you want to keep time for lunch, purchases or the train back.
howItWorks: From the station, head straight for the historic center instead of wasting time on longer detours.
The most balanced route combines the mother church, the rue and the main square.
If you plan a food break, choose somewhere near the return path or limit it to a quick stop at the bakery or dairy.

plans.label: Short stop
plans.bestFor: A first taste of the village
plans.summary: An essential loop through the main church, historic alleyways and a quick taste of local life.
plans.logistics: The best route stays compact: church, rue and the central square in one clean sequence.
plans.mealTip: Warm bread, baked goods and a drink in the center are the easiest options.

spring: Visit the Church of Sant'Eustachio and the first stretch of old lanes with carved portals and pale stone.
Make a fast bakery stop for village bread and simple traditional baked goods.
summer: Walk through the shaded rue up to Palazzo Nanni.
Take photos among stone houses, flowered balconies and the clean outline of the Majella.
autumn: Follow the perimeter of the fortified village, where the houses themselves still read like defensive walls.
Pause in Piazza Duval for chestnuts or other seasonal flavors.
winter: See Sant'Eustachio and the stone portals made even more striking by snow.
Warm up with hot chocolate or punch in a bar before heading back.

plans.label: Longer stop
plans.bestFor: A fuller village visit with a food break
plans.summary: A slower route that adds identity landmarks and a more deliberate pause.
plans.logistics: With two hours you can fit in old houses, the square and a targeted local purchase without rushing.
plans.mealTip: The dairy, the bakery or a table near the center work better than a scattered sit-down lunch.

spring: Explore Casa Quaranta, the medieval rue and the carved details on ancient portals.
Stop at the local dairy for pecorino and fresh ricotta.
summer: Climb toward the upper part of the village, where traces of the medieval castle still survive.
Browse a local products shop or taste a little genziana before sitting down.
autumn: Take a complete walk through the fortified village among stone views and mountain colors.
Enjoy a simple local lunch without leaving the center.
winter: Walk through the snowy center looking for the best portals and the most intimate passages of the rue.
Pause for Abruzzese sweets before returning to the train.

stops.name: Palena
stops.timeHint: The station is not in the village: the experience changes completely depending on whether a shuttle is provided.
stops.desc: In Quarto Santa Chiara the railway reaches one of its most scenic points: high altitude, silence and a village that can only be reached on certain runs.
stops.heroAlt: Mountain landscape near Palena on the edge of the Majella range.

schedule.departure: After the scenic stop or the shuttle transfer
schedule.duration: 40-60 min at the station, up to 90 min with the village
schedule.note: The station is at Valico della Forchetta, about 8-9 km above the town. In winter the stop usually remains up at the station.

seasonContext.spring: When the train includes the shuttle, this is the best time to combine nature, the wildlife area and hermitages.
seasonContext.summer: High altitude keeps temperatures low and allows a short but very scenic visit.
seasonContext.autumn: The foliage of Quarto Santa Chiara is one of the strongest reasons to step off the train even for a short stop.
seasonContext.winter: In winter Palena is above all a photographic stop: snow, wind and the historic train create the most iconic moment of the trip.

highlights: Quarto Santa Chiara and the Valico della Forchetta plateau.
Possible shuttle down to the village of Palena.
Brown Bear Wildlife Area.
Celestinian Hermitage of Madonna dell'Altare.
Ducal Castle, geopaleontological museum and Teatro Aventino.
stops.practicalNote: Logistics matter here more than anywhere else: if you stay at the station, focus on scenery, photos and a short break; if there is a shuttle, choose two clear stops in advance because your real time in the village will be shorter than the total stop.
howItWorks: Check whether your run includes only the scenic stop or the shuttle down to town.
In winter, treat the stop mainly as a high-altitude experience close to the train.
If you do go down to the village, focus on one clear axis: wildlife area, hermitage or castle and museum.

plans.label: Station stop
plans.summary: The most essential but also the most cinematic version of Palena, centered on the station itself.
plans.logistics: Staying near the station makes the best use of time and removes any dependence on transfers.
plans.mealTip: When they are available, the temporary stalls with hot drinks and sweets are the most practical pause.

spring: Take in the view over Quarto Santa Chiara and the first signs of the Majella park waking up.
Enjoy a short walk around the station and photograph the historic train inside the mountain basin.
summer: Pause in the cool mountain air among high meadows, light wind and the silence of the pass.
Use the stop for a photo session with the train and the broad landscape of one of the line's most scenic halts.
autumn: Watch the foliage spread across the plateau in yellows, reds and oranges around the station.
Take a small scenic break with local products if refreshment points are present.
winter: Snowy panorama and photographs of the train in the snow, perhaps the most iconic image of the whole day.
Hot tea or mulled wine at the temporary refreshment stalls, when available.

plans.label: Shuttle to the village
plans.summary: When the transfer is available, Palena adds castle, museum and spiritual landmarks tied to the Majella.
plans.logistics: Focus on two clear stops only: the village does not allow much wandering if you want to respect the train timings.
plans.mealTip: A short seated break or an easy local purchase works better than a full meal.

spring: Visit the Brown Bear Wildlife Area near the convent of Sant'Antonio.
Take a short outing toward the Hermitage of Madonna dell'Altare, linked to the memory of Pietro da Morrone.
summer: Explore the Ducal Castle and the Alto Aventino geopaleontological museum, which explains the ancient marine origins of these mountains.
Walk through the old center up to the tiny Teatro Aventino, intimate and surprising.
autumn: Wander through Palena's stone village among mountain honey, cheeses and apple sweets from the area.
Let the foliage of the Majella follow the whole visit between the village and the surrounding woods.
winter: If the shuttle is available even in colder weather, keep the visit limited to one central axis of the village.
Warm up indoors and enjoy a final panoramic glimpse before returning to the station.

stops.name: Roccaraso
stops.timeHint: There is enough margin here for lunch and a real walk: the stop supports a more relaxed rhythm.
stops.desc: Rebuilt after the war and now an elegant mountain resort, Roccaraso mixes historical memory, shopping streets, pine woods and strong seasonal tourism.
stops.heroAlt: View of Roccaraso between the town and the surrounding mountain slopes.

schedule.note: There is usually enough time to have lunch at ease and take a full walk through the center, the lawns or the pine wood.

seasonContext.spring: As the snow retreats, meadows and easy paths come back into play: the best moment to walk without rushing.
seasonContext.summer: Cooler air than the plains makes the pine wood, center and outdoor lunch especially pleasant.
seasonContext.autumn: Roccaraso changes register: woodland flavors, crisp light and the memory of reconstruction emerge more clearly.
seasonContext.winter: Here the railway meets the ski resort: snow, markets, hot sweets and a much livelier town center.

highlights: Piazza Leone and the rebuilt town center.
Via Roma with mountain shops and boutiques.
Pratone and Piazza Prato for an outdoor break.
Pineta Ombrellone and the adventure park.
San Michele hermitage and wartime memorials.
stops.practicalNote: Roccaraso lets you widen your radius more than the other stops: you can have lunch calmly and then decide whether to stay between the center and shopping streets or move toward the lawns and pine wood without forcing a long hike.
howItWorks: Treat lunch as part of the visit rather than as an interruption.
The center is linear, so Via Roma, the square, the church and the memorials read well in sequence.
If you choose the pine wood or the hermitage, keep the return route simple and avoid stacking too many moves.

plans.label: Short stop
plans.summary: An essential route that captures the refined and seasonal character of the resort.
plans.logistics: Focus on the center and squares, leaving trails and the pine wood to the longer stop.
plans.mealTip: An artisan pastry, a hot drink or a quick lunch near the center avoid wasting time.

spring: Pause at the Pratone and walk along Via Roma among shops, greenery and crisp mountain air.
If you want a viewpoint, make a short climb toward the Hermitage of San Michele.
summer: Walk through the center and take a quick break in Pineta Ombrellone away from the sun.
Go for a charcuterie board or a light lunch before heading back.
autumn: Follow Via Roma up to Santa Maria Assunta in clear autumn light.
Stop for woodland flavors such as porcini mushrooms, truffle or other local specialties.
winter: Enjoy Piazza Leone among decorations, snow and ski-season energy.
Choose a pastry shop or central bar for artisan sweets and warm drinks such as bombardino.

plans.label: Long stop
plans.bestFor: Relaxed lunch and a fuller walk
plans.summary: The stop that best combines mountain cuisine, green space and the area's historical memory.
plans.logistics: After lunch choose one extended axis: center with memorial stops or pine wood with outdoor activities.
plans.mealTip: With 2-3 hours it makes sense to sit down in a restaurant and close with a slow walk.

spring: Take a broader walk through the Pratone, the center and the mountain shops.
Follow the easy path toward the Hermitage of San Michele for a more open valley view.
summer: Spend time in Pineta Ombrellone, possibly adding the adventure park.
Enjoy an Abruzzese lunch and a relaxed walk through the rebuilt village.
autumn: Take a complete walk through the center, church and memorials that recall the Second World War.
Have lunch with woodland flavors such as porcini mushrooms or truffle.
winter: Choose between the G. Bolino ice rink or a snowy walk through the center.
Warm up with a proper lunch, hot chocolate, mountain desserts and richer local cooking.

stops.name: Castel di Sangro
stops.timeHint: One of the most comfortable stops: you can have lunch without rushing and still add a proper cultural visit.
stops.desc: Ancient Aufidena mixes river landscapes, museums, historic palaces and upper neighborhoods: this is the stop that best supports a complete itinerary.
stops.heroAlt: View of Castel di Sangro with the urban fabric following the Sangro valley.

schedule.note: The stop is long enough for lunch, the historic center and at least one museum or panoramic visit.

seasonContext.spring: Longer days make it natural to link the river, the convent and the upper Civita quarter.
seasonContext.summer: During the central hours it is better to alternate cloisters, museums and shaded alleys such as Terravecchia.
seasonContext.autumn: The Sangro river, its bridges and the foliage give the visit a slower and more photographic tone.
seasonContext.winter: A warm lunch, the pinacoteca and the main square are the best combination when temperatures drop.

highlights: Piazza Plebiscito and its monumental fountain.
Palazzo De Petra with the Teofilo Patini picture gallery.
Maddalena convent and the fly-fishing museum.
Civic Museum Aufidenate with Samnite and Roman finds.
Civita, Terravecchia and the Basilica of Santa Maria Assunta.
stops.practicalNote: Castel di Sangro truly allows you to combine lunch and culture: decide first whether to prioritize the river and the Maddalena complex or the upper quarters, so the afternoon does not feel fragmented.
howItWorks: Use lunch as a central pause and build the route around one main cultural pole.
The Sangro cycle path and the convent work well together; Civita, the basilica and Terravecchia form a second coherent axis.
Before departure, stay close to the center for easy purchases such as honey, aged cheeses or truffle specialties.

plans.label: Short stop
plans.bestFor: Historic center and one focused visit
plans.summary: A compact itinerary that keeps together the square, a historic palace and a short walk.
plans.logistics: Choose one pole only: square and palace, or river and convent, without trying to do everything.
plans.mealTip: A central trattoria or a quick pause in the square are the most efficient options.

spring: Walk along the Sangro river or the cycle path near the center.
Climb toward Civita for a first panoramic overlook above the valley.
summer: Visit the Maddalena convent, cooler and more shaded during the central hours.
Take a quick walk through Terravecchia among stone arches and narrow alleys.
autumn: Move through the historic center around Palazzo De Petra, Palazzo Balzano and the old quarter.
Pick up mountain honey, aged cheeses or truffle-based specialties before departure.
winter: Enjoy Piazza Plebiscito and a warm lunch in the heart of town.
Visit Palazzo De Petra with a quick stop at the Teofilo Patini picture gallery.

plans.label: Long stop
plans.bestFor: A full route with unhurried lunch
plans.summary: The ideal terminus for combining museums, historic quarters, the river and local cuisine.
plans.logistics: With more time you can include a museum and still close the route with Civita or the riverside path.
plans.mealTip: This is where it is worth sitting down properly: timings allow for a full lunch before boarding again.

spring: Visit the Maddalena convent and the International Fly Fishing Museum.
Take a long walk between the Sangro cycle path, Civita and the Basilica of Santa Maria Assunta.
summer: Explore the Maddalena complex with both the Aufidenate civic museum and the fly-fishing museum.
Finish with Terravecchia and the basilica for a wide panorama over the valley.
autumn: Visit the Civic Museum Aufidenate and walk along the Sangro among bridges, water and foliage.
Enjoy a calm lunch and do last-minute shopping for mountain products in the center.
winter: Pair a full traditional lunch with a cultural visit among the historic palace and the picture gallery.
Close with a final walk through the square among lights, fountain and terminus atmosphere.

info.subtitle: A practical guide to understand how departure works, what to expect on board and which details are worth checking before you book.
info.bookingAria: Open the official website for calendar and tickets in a new tab
info.tripadvisorAria: Open TripAdvisor reviews in a new tab
info.accessibilityAria: Write to request accessibility and assistance support

quickGuide.title: Before departure
quickGuide.body: The trip is easy to enjoy if you prepare well: a few smart checks prevent problems on departure day.

checklist: Get to the station at least 30 minutes before departure.
Dress in layers: the train can be warm, mountain stops often are not.
Bring water and snacks: there is no restaurant car.
Book well in advance during the busiest periods.

seasonHighlights.label: Winter
seasonHighlights.description: Snow, Christmas markets and a real mountain atmosphere.

seasonHighlights.label: Autumn
seasonHighlights.description: Foliage, clean light and excellent conditions for photos.

seasonHighlights.label: Spring and summer
seasonHighlights.description: Hikes, bikes, picnics and open-air stops.

rules.title: On-board rules
rules.body: A historic train needs a bit more care than a modern one: a few simple habits make the trip better for everyone.
items: Do not lean out of the windows while the train is moving.
Do not leave rubbish on board or along the line.
Handle the original wood, velvet and fittings with care.

arrival.title: Getting to Sulmona
arrival.body: Departure is designed for early arrivals, so knowing where to go and how to move around makes the morning much smoother.
tips: Departure is almost always from Sulmona station, usually platform 1.
There is convenient parking close to the station entrance, with more spaces along the avenue.
If you rely on regional trains from farther away, arriving the evening before can be the safer option.

support.title: Accessibility and assistance
support.body: For wheelchairs, reduced mobility or specific needs, it is better to ask first and book after you get confirmation.

categories.label: Planning
categories.description: Route, timings, tickets and how to arrive at the station prepared.

categories.label: On board
categories.description: Comfort, essential services and what to expect from vintage carriages.

categories.label: Accessibility
categories.description: Reduced mobility, strollers, animals and requests worth making before you book.

info.faqSubtitle: Key answers are organized by topic so you can find what matters before departure.
info.faqAria: Frequently asked questions on the useful info page
info.faqEmpty: No information is available for this category.

faq.q: Why is it called the Transiberiana?
faq.a: The nickname comes from the Abruzzo plateaus that in winter, with snow and wind, resemble Siberian landscapes. Today it is one of the best known names in Italian railway tourism.

It almost always leaves from Sulmona and climbs through the Majella and the Apennines, with stops that change by season and theme, often including Campo di Giove, Palena, Roccaraso and Castel di Sangro.
It is usually a full day excursion: morning departure, late afternoon return and long stops in the villages to visit, eat or join local events.

faq.q: When does it feel most special?
faq.a: Winter is best for snow and Christmas markets, autumn for foliage, spring and summer for hikes, bikes and outdoor stops. There is no single best season, only a different kind of trip.
Online on the official website or through partner operators. In the busiest periods seats sell out quickly, so it is worth watching the release dates and booking early.

faq.q: Where do I leave from and where can I park?
faq.a: The main departure point is Sulmona railway station. There is practical parking a short walk from the entrance, and arriving early makes the start of the day much easier.
You travel in historic 1920s and 1930s stock with original interiors, manual doors and openable windows. There is no Wi-Fi or power outlets: the vintage atmosphere is part of the appeal.
The carriages are heated, often very well. The real challenge is the mountain stops, so thermal layers, proper shoes and a warm jacket still matter.

faq.q: Do you eat on the train?
faq.a: There is no restaurant car. You can bring water and snacks, while lunch is usually handled during longer stops, as a packed meal, at market stalls or in village restaurants.
Yes, that is one of the main reasons people choose it. The landscapes are central to the experience, but always take photos safely and never lean out while moving.

faq.q: Could reduced mobility be a problem?
faq.a: It can be. Historic carriages have narrow entrances, steep steps and facilities not designed for modern accessibility needs, so it is important to check everything before booking.

faq.q: Can I travel with a wheelchair?
faq.a: If it folds, it can usually go in the luggage car, but if you need an accessible position during the trip you should ask in advance whether an equipped carriage is available.
Yes, but they cannot stay open in the corridors. They need to be folded and stored, and in the villages a baby carrier is often more practical than wheels.
Small dogs are generally allowed if kept in a carrier or in your arms. Guide dogs are always allowed; for any other case it is best to confirm the rules before booking.

decalogues.title: Environmental decalogue
decalogues.body: Ten concrete actions connecting home, territory and community: everyday sustainability, respect for parks and responsibility toward who comes after us.

items.title: The 3 Rs: reduce, reuse, recycle
items.body: Produce less waste, reuse what you can and sort recycling properly. Appliances and batteries always go to the recycling center.

items.title: Ditch single-use and keep beaches clean
items.body: Choose bottles and canvas bags. At the sea take your trash away and use a pocket ashtray: cigarette butts pollute water and harm fish.

items.title: Save water and energy at home
items.body: Turn off lights, use LEDs, avoid overheating rooms and close the tap when not needed. Never pour frying oil or paint down the drain.

items.title: Move smart
items.body: Walk, bike or take public transport more often: using the car less means cleaner air for everyone.

items.title: Buy local and seasonal food
items.body: Choose local products to cut transport and consider eating less meat: it saves water and lowers pollution.

items.title: Buy only what you need
items.body: Clothes and objects have an environmental cost. Buy the essentials, second-hand when possible, and avoid unnecessary plastic packaging.

items.title: Respect wilderness and parks
items.body: In National Parks stay on the trails, leave no waste and avoid loud noises or drones that disturb wildlife.

items.title: Think about the future
items.body: It is not enough to avoid damage: we also need to care for nature so we hand it down in good health.

items.title: Do your part and speak up
items.body: If you see trash or pollution, report it to authorities. Join clean-up days for beaches and parks when you can.

items.title: Be a steward, not an owner
items.body: Real progress is living in harmony with Earth, respecting its balances instead of trying to dominate it.

decalogues.title: Decalogue for digital sustainability
decalogues.body: Technology and environment intersect more than it seems: hardware, energy, printing, data and online habits can all be lighter and more mindful.

items.title: Recycle and dispose of electronics correctly
items.body: E-waste contains valuable materials but also toxins: take it to recycling centers or stores that collect used devices, never to mixed trash.

items.title: Use your devices longer
items.body: The biggest impact of phones and PCs is production. Before replacing them, try repairing and resist the race to the latest model.

items.title: Turn off what you are not using
items.body: Standby and plugged-in chargers consume energy. Shut down the computer if idle for long and unplug what you do not need.

items.title: Choose efficient, sustainable devices
items.body: When you must buy, check energy efficiency and consider refurbished gear: it extends product life and avoids new production.

items.title: Be mindful of printing
items.body: Print only when necessary. If you must, use double-sided, reduce margins and prefer black and white.

items.title: Choose eco-friendly search engines
items.body: Alternatives like Ecosia allocate part of profits to reforestation or renewables.

items.title: Limit doomscrolling
items.body: Endless scrolling wastes time, data and energy. Some digital detox helps both you and the planet.

items.title: Bookmark frequent sites
items.body: If you visit the same sites often, save them: you avoid repeated searches and unnecessary data traffic.

items.title: Use AI thoughtfully
items.body: Generating text or images with AI needs a lot of computing power. Use it when it truly adds value, not for trivial requests.

items.title: Raise awareness
items.body: Digital pollution is hard to see: sharing these practices at school, with family and friends multiplies the positive impact.

meteo.title: Live weather
meteo.subtitle: Real-time weather conditions along the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo.

empty.title: No data available
empty.dark: The station sensors are sleeping deeply. The weather owl is keeping watch. Try again in a few minutes.
empty.light: The station sensors are sleeping. Try again in a few minutes, the weather bunny is on the job.

error.title: Connection lost
error.dark: The candle has gone out, we cannot reach the sensors. Check the connection and try again.
error.light: We cannot reach the IoT sensors. Check the connection and try again.

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.body: The page you are looking for does not exist or has been moved. You can go back to the home page and continue the journey.
